title: AI That Heals: Healthcare Explained

modules.title: How AI Reads Medical Images
modules.sub: Decoding how algorithms detect patterns in scans and slides
modules.description: Learners examine how convolutional neural networks analyze X-rays, MRIs, and pathology slides to flag anomalies. They compare AI-assisted reads against traditional radiologist workflows and assess where automation adds speed or introduces error. By the end, learners can articulate the clinical value and the failure modes of image-recognition tools in diagnostic settings.

modules.title: AI in Drug Discovery Today
modules.sub: Tracing AI's role from molecule screening to clinical trials
modules.description: Learners explore how machine learning accelerates target identification, compound screening, and toxicity prediction in pharmaceutical pipelines. They review real-world cases where AI-designed molecules entered trials and weigh the gap between promising models and approved drugs. This module builds a realistic picture of where AI genuinely shortens timelines and where human expertise remains irreplaceable.

modules.title: Predicting Disease Before Symptoms
modules.sub: Using risk models and biomarkers to get ahead of illness
modules.description: Learners investigate how predictive algorithms use electronic health records, genetic data, and wearable signals to flag high-risk patients before symptoms appear. They evaluate the statistical logic behind early-warning scores and confront the ethical questions around acting on probabilistic predictions. The module asks learners to weigh clinical benefit against the psychological and social costs of predictive labeling.

modules.title: Robot-Assisted Surgery Basics
modules.sub: Understanding what robotic systems do inside the operating room
modules.description: Learners get a grounded overview of how systems like the da Vinci platform translate a surgeon's hand movements into precise instrument actions. They distinguish between fully autonomous robotics and teleoperated assistance, examining the evidence on outcomes, complications, and learning curves. The module helps learners form an informed view of when robotic assistance genuinely improves patient results.

modules.title: AI in Mental Health Support
modules.sub: Examining chatbots, monitoring tools, and their real limits
modules.description: Learners survey AI-powered mental health applications ranging from conversational agents to passive mood-monitoring apps, assessing the clinical evidence behind each. They grapple with the tension between expanding access to support and the risks of replacing human therapeutic relationships with algorithmic ones. The module challenges learners to define appropriate boundaries for AI in emotionally sensitive care contexts.

modules.title: Patient Data and Privacy Risks
modules.sub: Mapping how health data flows, leaks, and gets exploited
modules.description: Learners trace the lifecycle of patient data from point of care through cloud storage, third-party analytics, and potential breach scenarios. They apply key regulatory frameworks such as HIPAA and GDPR to real healthcare AI deployments, identifying compliance gaps and governance weaknesses. By the end, learners can spot the privacy vulnerabilities most commonly introduced when AI systems are integrated into clinical infrastructure.

modules.title: Bias in Medical AI Systems
modules.sub: Identifying how skewed data produces unequal patient outcomes
modules.description: Learners analyze documented cases where medical AI systems performed significantly worse for women, people of color, or lower-income populations due to unrepresentative training data. They work through the technical and institutional mechanisms that allow bias to enter models and go undetected through validation. The module equips learners to ask the right audit questions before endorsing or deploying a clinical AI tool.

modules.title: The Future of AI Care
modules.sub: Anticipating where AI medicine is headed and what it means
modules.description: Learners synthesize trends across the course to project near-term developments in AI-assisted care, from ambient clinical documentation to personalized treatment recommendation engines. They weigh competing scenarios for the evolving role of clinicians alongside AI, considering workforce, liability, and trust implications. The module closes by asking learners to define the conditions under which AI in healthcare genuinely serves patients rather than just systems.

synopsis: Explore how artificial intelligence is transforming hospitals, clinics, and personal wellness from diagnosis to treatment. This course unpacks real tools, honest limitations, and the human stakes behind AI-powered medicine.
rationale: With a demand score of 7/10 and low corpus similarity of 0.61 to a general-purpose course like 'AI que Ves Cada Día', a dedicated healthcare AI course fills a critical gap for learners seeking relevant, sector-specific AI literacy in one of the highest-impact fields.
